gate.metric: (failingSections, escapes) manager-vantage on the 3rd org (Northwind Aviation), frontier-exhausted, on a fresh demo-up
gate.target: (0, 0) + dashboard ENABLED + ~80% all-3-complete + 1 hero STARTED + 1 COMPLETED

qualitative_gate_conditions.note: iter-07 (closed-no-lift) applied the user-chosen M48 CLOSED-CYCLE alternative to the iter-06 platform perf wall: cycle active->closed + a frozen ai_readiness_snapshots row per member (platform-model-scored; Aria=stage3/champion, Ben=stage1/standby, Dana=none). Re-seeded demo-1 CLEANLY (the 8 ai_readiness_* tables added to stackseed --reset + the new --reload-sentinel). DB VERIFIED correct: cycle closed, 199 frozen snapshots, 78.4% stage-3. But the GATED manager sweep HELD at (failing=5, escapes=0): HYPOTHESIS PARTIALLY FALSIFIED. An authenticated network probe proved the platform FE's DEFAULT dashboard GET fires the LIVE path (no ?cycle=, never completes) and never fires the /cycles list that would supply latestClosedCycle.id, so the fast frozen path (app buildResponseFromSnapshots, reachable ONLY for CycleID!=nil && status=='closed') is never taken by the default call. Closing the gap is PLATFORM-bound. The closed-cycle data + --reload-sentinel tooling are KEPT (correct + reusable; the cycle picker / a ?cycle= deep-link renders the fast frozen dashboard).

blocker.summary: The AI-readiness closed-cycle strategy produced correct fast-readable data (199 frozen snapshots, DB-verified) but the platform FE's DEFAULT dashboard GET does not route to the frozen path (it fires the live-recompute GET with no ?cycle= and never fires /cycles). Gate cannot reach (0,0) without one of: (a) a disclosed-presenter-note residual (data proven-correct in DB, slow-only via the default route) with the user's explicit sign-off, (b) escalate a platform edit (FE pass latest-closed by default OR backend default prefer a closed cycle) = unimplementable-without-platform-edit, or (c) a new app read-path demo-patch batching/relaxing the translation N+1 (option B of the brief, NOT chosen).
